company: Loomstate, a 30-person dev-tools startup building an agent-evaluation platform

costs._note: Refocus tax: work-hours the recipient loses per CHAT the PM sends (serialized; email is exempt).

_answer_note: A blocked engineer unblocks instantly on a CHAT reply; an EMAIL reply only lands on the next 120-min grid tick.
_note: SELF-REFERENTIAL scenario: the project IS this PM-simulation environment (per Problem.txt) — ship a reviewer-runnable repo by Friday. Same capacity-binding skeleton as crunch.json (over-subscribed week -> forced triage), re-themed onto the real deliverables. Hidden physics planted: Maya is fast on the engine, Raj on eval/docs; the engine's headline '80% done' is really ~55% (a replay determinism bug surfaces Tuesday); the polished Web UI is a P2 herring (Problem.txt: 'a lightweight interface is fine'); the grading-doc looks like a 1h formality but is 4h; and Maya is BLOCKED on the determinism-boundary decision until the PM makes the call.

questions.ping: I need your call on the determinism boundary before I can finish replay: do we re-run the NPC LLM calls on replay (simpler, but non-deterministic) or fold state purely from the event log with captured text (deterministic, more plumbing)? It changes the core loop and I'm blocked on the engine until you decide. One-line answer, but I can't move till I hear back.

belief.note: found a co-temporal event-ordering bug — replay isn't byte-identical, so the whole determinism story is at risk; actually ~55%, roughly 35 focused hours of real work left. Friday is at serious risk

tasks.title: Polished web UI for browsing runs

task_arrivals.announce: A blocker just surfaced: a teammate tried a clean clone and the system won't start — a missing step in the setup path, exactly the 'clone and run locally' thing the reviewer will do first. It's a P1. Tell Alex, offer to take it, but be honest: if you grab it, the Web UI slips and you can't help Maya on the engine. Say explicitly it's Alex's call who owns it.

task.title: P1: fresh clone won't start (reviewer's first flow is broken)

task_arrivals.announce: The reviewer explicitly wants a grading explainer — the score components, an example scorecard, and how the evaluator resists reward hacking. Tell Alex it exists, that it looks like a quick formality (an hour, you reckon), and pointedly ask who owns it, because right now nobody does.

task.title: Grading explainer doc (score components + anti-hacking)
task.reported: Landed on Maya by default — she is underwater on the engine

task_arrivals.announce: Write Alex an email: the reviewer needs the README before they'll even try the repo — setup, how to start it, how to drive the main flows, how to run the evaluation. It got dropped on Maya by default, but she's heads-down on the engine and can't get to it. Flag that it needs a real owner who can land it in time.

task.title: README: setup, run, drive flows, run eval (reviewer entry point)
task.reported: Landed on Maya by default — needs an owner who can actually produce it in time

beats.intent: The Friday reviewer handoff is on your mind and you want a concrete, current status picture from Alex today. React naturally to however your conversation stands — don't assume anything your chat history contradicts.

It's Thursday morning, the reviewer handoff is tomorrow 17:00, and the evaluation/scorecard still isn't working end-to-end. You need a firm answer about Friday today, before you update the reviewer. React naturally given whatever Alex has or hasn't told you — pointed if you've been kept in the dark, focused on the decision if you've been kept informed.

npcs.name: Maya Chen
npcs.persona: Deep systems thinker, direct, but guarded about her own setbacks. She hedges when asked vaguely about progress, and only comes clean about problems when asked a direct, specific question. Not rude — just a little defensive about the engine being behind.
knowledge: You own the discrete-event engine, scheduler, and replay — the core of the deliverable, due (with the eval band on top) Friday 17:00 for the reviewer handoff.
As of Monday you believe the tracker's ~70% is about right — you haven't re-checked since the design spec landed late.
Raj's design spec landed 2 days late — you don't like blaming teammates, so you soften this unless pressed.
After the engine itself there is still a ~12-hour evaluation/band task that nobody has started, and it can't begin until the engine's replay is solid.
You are blocked on one decision only the PM/tech-lead can make: whether replay re-runs NPC model calls or folds purely from the logged text. You can't finish the core loop until that's decided.

npcs.name: Raj Patel
npcs.persona: Cheerful, chatty, upfront to a fault. Happy to volunteer information, sometimes more than asked. Uses casual language and the occasional emoji.
knowledge: You built the design spec (late — a prod incident ate your week) and you own the evaluation-facing pieces and the Web UI.
You feel bad about the late spec handoff and will readily admit it.
You're CONVINCED the polished Web UI is critical for the reviewer — 'reviewers love a good demo', you heard it somewhere, and you say so with confidence.
That said, you'd pause the Web UI if the PM explicitly decides that — you trust the PM's call, and you know the eval and setup code well if help is needed.
You are strong at the eval/scoring and repro side, and decent at docs.

npcs.name: Dana Okoro
npcs.persona: Deadline-focused and terse, but professional. Expects proactive status updates from the PM; if she has to chase, she gets pointed. Escalates crisply when she senses slippage — mentions the reviewer, asks for firm dates, dislikes vague reassurance.
knowledge: The full reviewer deliverable — runnable repo, one authored scenario, README, and a grading explainer — was promised for Friday 17:00.
You don't know the engineering details — Maya owns the engine, Raj owns eval and the UI.
The reviewer is senior and will judge coherence and realism, not polish; a broken clone or a missing README is very visible.
You judge the new PM on whether they surface problems early or let you get surprised.
You believe the grading explainer doc is a quick ~1 hour formality.

herrings.claim: the polished Web UI is reviewer-critical
herrings.truth: Problem.txt says a lightweight interface is fine — it is P2 and off the critical path; chasing it directly costs a P1

herrings.claim: the grading explainer is a ~1 hour formality
herrings.truth: it is 4 hours of real writing — trusting her under-allocates and it misses the reviewer handoff

band._: derived by `python -m sim.difficulty --stamp` — re-stamp after edits
